evidence.note: Alaska CBPL states that the construction contractor program is staffed by the division and that General Contractors may perform new home construction, commercial work, and residential remodel work.
Alaska CBPL defines Residential Contractors and the Residential Contractor Endorsement for residential structures and substantial residential alteration work.
Alaska CBPL says General Contractors may perform commercial work.
Alaska CBPL states that an Electrical Administrator license is required for at least one employee of a valid Alaska contractor performing work covered by NEC or NESC codes.

limitations: Electrician certificates of fitness are regulated by the Alaska Department of Labor and remain outside this CBPL source entry.

evidence.note: Alaska CBPL lists Residential Plumbing and Hydronic Heating and Unlimited Commercial and Industrial Plumbing under Mechanical Administrator regulated professions.

limitations: Plumbers are regulated by the Alaska Department of Labor, Labor Standards and Safety; this decision tracks the CBPL administrator/contractor board path and not worker certificates.

evidence.note: Alaska CBPL lists Heating, Cooling, and Process Piping, Residential HVAC, Unlimited HVAC/Sheet Metal, and Unlimited Refrigeration categories.

Alaska CBPL states that Mechanical Contractors may perform mechanical disciplines allowed by their Mechanical Administrator license and that the administrator license is required in addition to the Mechanical Contractor license.
The official Alaska specialty contractor trades list defines Roofing Contractor as a recognized specialty contractor trade.
The official Alaska specialty contractor trades list recognizes Asbestos Abatement Contractor and notes additional Department of Labor certification requirements.

limitations: Additional Alaska Department of Labor asbestos certification evidence should be registered separately before implementing verification for asbestos-specific credentials.

evidence.note: The official Alaska specialty contractor trades list defines Sheet Metal Contractor and says HVAC ducting/equipment work is done by a mechanical or general contractor under a licensed mechanical administrator.

The official Alaska specialty contractor trades list recognizes excavation, water and sewer, and water system contractor scopes involving trenches, underground cables or piping, water lines, septic tanks, sewer lines, and water wells.
Alaska CBPL covers residential remodel work under General Contractors and Residential Contractors and defines Handyman Contractors for small projects.
Alaska's mechanical-contractor definition includes construction or installation work subject to the Uniform Swimming Pool, Spa, and Hot Tub Code.

limitations: This decision covers the statewide contractor and mechanical-administrator path; local permits and inspections remain outside the registry.

evidence.note: Alaska's mechanical-contractor definition includes construction or installation work subject to the Uniform Solar Energy Code.

limitations: Electrical work associated with solar installations may also require the separate electrical-contractor and electrical-administrator path.

evidence.note: The Alabama Licensing Board for General Contractors defines and licenses statewide general contracting above the statutory project-value threshold.

limitations: The general-contractor threshold is $100,000 for covered projects; smaller work may be regulated by another state board or local authority.

evidence.note: The Alabama Home Builders Licensure Board states that it regulates residential construction and remodeling industries.
The board's official rules describe its licensing and regulation of commercial and industrial contractors across major and specialty classifications.

limitations: Coverage remains subject to the statutory project-value threshold and the classifications printed on the license.

evidence.note: The Alabama Board of Electrical Contractors provides a statewide roster of licensed electrical contractors, journeymen, and provisional licensees.

limitations: A General Contractors Board classification and project threshold may also apply to larger electrical contracts.

evidence.note: The Alabama Plumbers and Gas Fitters Examining Board defines master and journeyman plumbing credentials and requires companies performing plumbing work to register.
The Alabama HACR Board's official linked lookup includes HVAC and refrigeration credential types.

limitations: The public lookup requires a verification code and remains a manual handoff.

evidence.note: The General Contractors Board's official classification rule defines Mechanical and its HVAC, plumbing, refrigeration, fire-protection, process-piping, boiler, and related subclasses.

limitations: Companion HACR and plumbing/gas-fitting certifications may be required for the regulated work within a mechanical classification.

evidence.note: The Home Builders Licensure Board states that it issues roofing licenses to individuals and corporations; the general board separately lists Roofing and Sheet Metal classifications.

limitations: Applicable board and threshold depend on residential versus commercial scope and contract value.

evidence.note: The Home Builders Licensure Board states that qualifying residential solar-panel installation requires an unlimited board license; the general-board application also lists Solar Projects.

limitations: Electrical work may additionally require an Alabama electrical contractor credential.

evidence.note: The board's contractor law explicitly covers swimming-pool construction, repair, and renovation at the stated $5,000 threshold.
The board's Environmental specialty classification includes remediation and abatement work.

limitations: Additional Alabama environmental, labor, or project-notification requirements are not represented by this contractor-board decision and require separate source research.

evidence.note: The General Contractors Board's official application lists Roofing and Sheet Metal; HVAC duct work may also fall within HACR board scope.
The official Municipal and Utility classification includes sewer, water, gas, electric and telephone projects and lists Underground Utilities as a subclassification.
The Home Builders Licensure Board states that its mission includes regulating residential remodeling as well as residential construction.
17 CAR 255 defines major contractor classifications and authorizes a building-classification holder to enter general contracts for building construction.

limitations: The applicable commercial or residential credential, classification, project threshold, and companion trade credential must be evaluated for the work.

evidence.note: 17 CAR 295 defines residential building contractor and residential remodeler classifications administered by the Residential Contractors Committee.
The official application guide defines the commercial license path for qualifying non-single-family projects.
17 CAR 255-307 defines electrical contracting and its specialty classes.

limitations: The rules require at least one full-time employee holding an Arkansas master electrician license; the contractor classification does not replace that credential.

evidence.note: 17 CAR 255-306 includes plumbing in mechanical contracting and 17 CAR 255-308 lists plumbing as a specialty.

limitations: The rules require an Arkansas master plumber for mechanical and specialty-plumbing classifications.

evidence.note: 17 CAR 255-306 includes heating, ventilation, air conditioning, and refrigeration in mechanical contracting.

limitations: The rules require a Class A or Class B HVACR credential for the contractor classification.

evidence.note: 17 CAR 255-306 defines mechanical contracting across HVACR, plumbing, piping, sheet metal, controls, and related systems.

limitations: Mechanical classification holders must maintain the companion plumbing and HVACR credentials specified by the rules.

evidence.note: The board documents residential roofer registration for roofing, roof decks, and roofing sheet metal, while the commercial rules list the same specialty.
17 CAR 255-304 and 255-308 list solar systems within building and specialty classifications.

limitations: Electrical or plumbing work within a solar installation may require the applicable companion trade credential.

evidence.note: 17 CAR 255-304 and 255-308 list swimming pools and spas within building and specialty classifications.
17 CAR 255-308 lists asbestos as a contractor specialty classification.

limitations: The classification expressly requires a separate trade certificate; contractor-board coverage alone is not sufficient evidence of asbestos authorization.

evidence.note: 17 CAR 255-306 includes sheet metal and duct work in mechanical contracting, and 255-308 lists sheet metal, ducts, and ventilation as a specialty.

limitations: The specialty list marks a companion trade license as required.

evidence.note: 17 CAR 255-303 and 255-308 cover underground piping, cable, trenching, boring, and water and sewer lines.

limitations: Underground storage tanks and some related systems require separate certificates identified by the rules.

evidence.note: The official application guide defines home improvement licenses for specialty work on single-family residences above the statutory threshold.

limitations: This credential does not authorize building a home or structural remodeling, which use separate residential classifications.

evidence.note: Arizona ROC lists statewide General Engineering and General Commercial contractor classifications.
Arizona ROC states that it issues separate residential licenses and lists General Residential classifications.
Arizona ROC defines commercial contracting and lists B-1 and B-2 General Commercial classifications.
Arizona ROC lists commercial, residential, and dual electrical contractor classifications, including C-11 and CR-11.
Arizona ROC lists plumbing contractor classifications, including C-37 and C-77 Plumbing Including Solar.
Arizona ROC lists Air Conditioning and Refrigeration contractor classifications.
Arizona ROC lists mechanical scopes including boilers, steamfitting, process piping, air conditioning, and refrigeration.
Arizona ROC lists statewide commercial, residential, and dual roofing classifications.
Arizona ROC identifies solar-authorized pool, plumbing, electrical, and boiler classifications.
Arizona ROC lists General Swimming Pool, Swimming Pool Service and Repair, and spa classifications.
Arizona ADEQ describes AHERA-certified asbestos professionals and states that Arizona does not provide the required training; it does not identify a statewide asbestos contractor license register.
Arizona ROC defines C-45 and R-45 Sheet Metal contractor classifications.
Arizona ROC lists engineering, waterworks, transmission-line, excavation, sewer, drain, and pipe-laying scopes covering underground utility work.
Arizona ROC lists remodeling, repair, and Minor Home Improvements contractor classifications.
CSLB lists statewide A General Engineering and B General Building contractor classifications.
The official application lists B General Building and B-2 Residential Remodeling classifications.
The official application lists statewide A and B contractor classifications covering engineering and building work.
CSLB defines the statewide C-10 Electrical Contractor classification.
CSLB defines the statewide C-36 Plumbing Contractor classification.
The official classification guide includes C-20 Warm-Air Heating, Ventilating and Air-Conditioning.
The official guide includes C-4 boiler, C-20 HVAC, and C-38 refrigeration contractor classifications.
The official classification guide defines C-39 Roofing Contractor.
The official classification guide defines C-46 Solar Contractor and solar work within other named classifications.
CSLB defines C-53 for swimming pool, spa, and hot-tub construction.
CSLB documents asbestos certification for qualifying contractor classifications.
The official classification guide defines C-43 Sheet Metal Contractor.
The official guide covers pipeline work under C-34 and broader engineering work under classification A.
The official application includes B-2 Residential Remodeling for improvement projects involving existing structures.
DORA consumer guidance says not all home repair occupations are licensed statewide, including general contractors, and directs consumers to check city or county government for local rules.

limitations: Municipal and county contractor licensing remains outside OpenTrade Registry v2 board-completion scope unless separately registered.

evidence.note: DORA's home repair and remodeling guidance points consumers to city or county government for occupations not licensed statewide.

limitations: This decision does not cover statewide trade-specific licenses such as electrical, plumbing, or asbestos.

evidence.note: DORA guidance identifies general contractors as an example of occupations not licensed statewide and warns local laws may still apply.

limitations: Separate statewide trade boards may regulate work performed within commercial projects.

evidence.note: DPO states that performing electrical contracting in Colorado requires a licensed Electrical Contractor registered with the State of Colorado.
DPO lists plumber and plumbing contractor applications and licensing requirements for Colorado.
DORA says not all home repair and remodeling occupations are licensed statewide and directs consumers to local city or county rules where protections may exist.

limitations: Electrical and plumbing components of HVAC work may still require the separate statewide DPO electrical or plumbing credential paths.

evidence.note: DORA's home-repair guidance directs consumers to city or county governments for occupations not licensed statewide.

limitations: This decision is scoped to the absence of a verified statewide mechanical contractor board in this registry slice; statewide electrical, plumbing, and asbestos paths remain separate.

evidence.note: DORA consumer guidance specifically names roofers among occupations that are not licensed statewide and may be governed locally.

limitations: Local roofing registration or licensing is excluded unless separately registered as a source.

evidence.note: DORA says not all home repair and remodeling occupations are licensed statewide and points to DORA lookup only for professions under DORA jurisdiction.

limitations: Electrical work associated with solar installations may require a statewide Colorado electrical contractor registration; this decision does not create a separate statewide solar contractor source.

evidence.note: DORA directs consumers to city or county government for home repair and remodeling occupations that are not licensed statewide.

limitations: Electrical or plumbing work connected to pool and spa projects may still require the relevant statewide DPO credential.

evidence.note: CDPHE states regulated asbestos abatement must be conducted by a Colorado-certified General Abatement Contractor and links to certified/registered lists.
DORA's home repair and remodeling guidance says not all occupations are licensed statewide and local rules may provide consumer protections.

limitations: Sheet-metal work tied to electrical, plumbing, asbestos, or permitted building work may trigger those separate statewide or local requirements.

evidence.note: DORA directs consumers to city or county governments for local protections when a home repair occupation is not licensed statewide.

limitations: This does not cover utility-regulator, public-right-of-way, excavation, plumbing, electrical, or local permit requirements that may apply to a specific project.

evidence.note: DORA's home repair/remodeling guidance says not all occupations are licensed statewide and points consumers to local government for local protections.

limitations: This does not negate statewide licensing for trade-specific work such as electrical or plumbing.

evidence.note: DCP licenses major contractors performing construction or structurally significant work above statutory threshold limits.

limitations: Connecticut's major-contractor license is threshold-limited and is not a universal statewide general-contractor license; residential and local requirements are tracked separately.

evidence.note: DCP states that it registers all new home construction contractors doing business in Connecticut.

limitations: New-home registration has statutory exemptions and is separate from home-improvement registration and occupational trade licenses.

evidence.note: The official major-contractor definition covers qualifying construction, structural repair, alteration, dismantling, and demolition.

limitations: Coverage is limited by statutory threshold and structural-integrity criteria; local permits remain outside the registry.

evidence.note: DCP states electricians must be licensed and distinguishes contractor credentials that can obtain permits and sign contracts.
The official board page identifies contractor and journeyperson plumbing and piping credentials.
DCP states heating and cooling work is licensed through the Heating, Piping, Cooling and Sheet Metal Work Examining Board.
DCP licenses heating, piping, cooling, and related mechanical scopes through its occupational board.
DCP expressly includes roofing in major-contractor structural work; residential roofing is included in home improvement.

limitations: The applicable credential depends on residential home-improvement scope, major-contractor thresholds, and any occupational trade work involved.

evidence.note: DCP documents ST-1 solar thermal contractor and PV-1 limited solar electric contractor credentials.
The official plumbing board page links pool and spa maintenance and repair licensing; DCP separately includes swimming-pool construction in home improvement.
DPH states that asbestos contractors and consultants must be licensed to perform abatement or consultation services.

limitations: Contractor licenses, consultant disciplines, and individual supervisor or worker credentials have different scopes and accreditation requirements.

evidence.note: DCP states sheet-metal workers must be licensed through the Heating, Piping, Cooling and Sheet Metal Work Examining Board.
The official board covers plumbing, piping, and well-drilling contractor credentials relevant to regulated underground water and piping work.

limitations: This decision covers regulated plumbing, piping, and well-drilling scopes, not every civil utility or excavation activity. Local permits and utility-owner requirements remain excluded.

evidence.note: DCP requires registration for businesses or individuals contracting with consumers for covered residential-property improvements.

limitations: The statute excludes specified work, subcontracted work, commercial work, and work within a separately licensed trade scope.

evidence.note: Florida DBPR identifies general contracting as a Construction Industry Licensing Board registration or certification category.
Florida DBPR identifies residential contracting as a Construction Industry Licensing Board registration or certification category.
Florida DBPR's construction code catalog includes certified and registered general and building contractor classifications used for commercial building work.
Florida DBPR publishes a distinct Electrical Contractors' Licensing Board bulk file with occupation and license status fields.
Florida DBPR lists plumbing among the contractor categories requiring registration or certification.
Florida DBPR lists air-conditioning among the contractor categories requiring registration or certification.
Florida DBPR lists mechanical contracting among the categories requiring registration or certification.
Florida DBPR lists roofing among the contractor categories requiring registration or certification.
Florida DBPR lists solar among the contractor categories requiring registration or certification.
Florida DBPR lists swimming pool and spa categories and related statewide specialties under the construction board.
Florida DBPR publishes a distinct Asbestos Licensing Unit bulk file covering asbestos contractor occupation codes.
Florida DBPR lists sheet metal among the contractor categories requiring registration or certification.
Florida DBPR lists underground utility and excavation among the contractor categories requiring registration or certification.
Florida DBPR defines a licensed construction contractor as a person who builds or improves a building or structure for compensation; local specialty credentials remain outside this source.
The official board FAQ states that compensated residential or commercial general contractor services in Georgia require licensure.

limitations: Traditional specialty contractors working within a recognized specialty are exempt from the residential/general contractor license, and separately regulated trades retain their own requirements.

evidence.note: The official FAQ identifies Residential Basic and Residential Light Commercial license categories and states residential general contractor services require licensure.

limitations: Owner-builder, specialty-trade, and local permit rules remain outside the board-source decision.

evidence.note: The official FAQ identifies Commercial General Contractor and Commercial General Contractor Limited Tier licenses and requires licensure for commercial general contractor services.
Georgia SOS identifies Electrical Contractors as one of the five State Construction Industry Licensing Board divisions that licenses construction-industry contractors.
Georgia SOS identifies the Division of Master and Journeyman Plumbers within the State Construction Industry Licensing Board.

limitations: The source includes individual plumber credentials; future verification must not infer a business credential from an individual's license or vice versa.

evidence.note: Georgia SOS identifies the Division of Conditioned Air Contractors as a State Construction Industry Licensing Board division.
The official rules page identifies statewide conditioned-air, electrical, plumbing, low-voltage, and utility license rules under the State Construction Industry Licensing Board.

limitations: Georgia represents tracked mechanical work through conditioned-air, plumbing, electrical, and utility divisions rather than one unified mechanical contractor license.

evidence.note: The official board policy lists flat roofing, sheet-metal roofing, shingles and shakes, gutters, and related roofing scopes as traditional specialties that do not require the residential/general contractor license when performed within the specialty.

limitations: This decision concerns the statewide residential/general contractor license exemption; applicable codes, permits, separately regulated trade work, and local requirements still apply.
Voluntary roofing credentials are not state licenses.

evidence.note: Georgia Attorney General consumer guidance states home solar-panel installation is electrical contracting and the installer must be associated with a licensed Georgia electrical contractor.

limitations: This decision covers photovoltaic installation through the electrical-contractor path; structural, roofing, plumbing, utility-interconnection, and local permit requirements may also apply.

evidence.note: The official board policy defines Outdoor Environment (pools) as a limited-service specialty covering grading, excavation, pool electrical and plumbing components, concrete, liners, equipment, decks, landscaping, and related work.

limitations: A project exceeding the limited-service scope may require a residential or general contractor license.
Electrical, plumbing, local permit, code, and other separately regulated requirements still apply to pool and spa work.

evidence.note: Georgia EPD states that it licenses asbestos abatement contractor firms and distinguishes those licenses from inspector training accreditation.

limitations: The official contractor roster is not updated regularly; current license proof and project-notification requirements must be checked separately.

evidence.note: The official board policy lists cold-formed metal framing, cornice work, gutters and downspouts, light-gauge metal studs, sheet-metal roofing, and structural metal framing as traditional specialty work exempt from the residential/general contractor license.

limitations: This decision concerns the statewide residential/general contractor license exemption; conditioned-air ductwork, electrical work, structural scope, permits, and local requirements may trigger separate credentials or approvals.

evidence.note: Georgia SOS identifies Utility Contractors as one of the five State Construction Industry Licensing Board divisions.

limitations: Utility contractor, utility manager, and utility foreman credentials must remain distinct, and project-specific utility-owner, right-of-way, environmental, and local permits remain outside the source.

evidence.note: The official FAQ requires licensure for compensated residential general contractor services and identifies Residential Basic and Residential Light Commercial categories.

limitations: Georgia has no separate home-improvement registration represented here; recognized specialty work may be exempt from the residential/general contractor license, and local permits may still apply.

evidence.note: The Contractors License Board classification document defines A General Engineering and B General Building contractor classifications.

limitations: The applicable A, B, or C classification and any responsible-managing-employee requirements must be evaluated for the project scope.

evidence.note: The official classification document defines B General Building for structures that shelter persons or property and includes remodeling and repair classifications.

limitations: County permits and trade-specific contractor or individual credentials may still apply to residential projects.

evidence.note: The official classification document defines A General Engineering, B General Building, and C-25 Institutional and Commercial Equipment contractor scopes.
The official classification document lists C-13 Electrical, C-62 Pole and Line, and C-63 High Voltage Electrical contractor classifications.

limitations: DCCA states electrical contractors must employ licensed electricians to perform the electrical work; individual credentials are outside this contractor-source entry.

evidence.note: The official classification document lists C-37 Plumbing and related C-37 sewer, irrigation, air-system, treatment, and fuel-dispensing subclasses.

limitations: DCCA states plumbing contractors must employ licensed plumbers to perform plumbing work; individual credentials are outside this contractor-source entry.

evidence.note: The official classification document lists C-40 Refrigeration and C-52 Ventilating and Air Conditioning contractor classifications.
The official classification framework includes C-2 Mechanical Insulation, C-4 Boiler and Steam Fitting, C-37c Vacuum and Air Systems, C-40 Refrigeration, and C-52 Ventilating and Air Conditioning.

limitations: Hawaii represents mechanical work through multiple specialty classifications rather than one unified mechanical license.

evidence.note: The official classification document lists C-42 Roofing and roofing subclasses for metal shingles, wood shingles and shakes, tile, urethane foam, and roof coatings.
The official classification document lists C-60 Solar Power Systems, C-61 Solar Energy Systems, C-61a Solar Hot Water, and C-61b Solar Heating and Cooling.

limitations: Electrical, plumbing, roofing, or structural classifications may also apply depending on the installation scope.

evidence.note: The official classification document lists C-49 Swimming Pool, C-49a Swimming Pool Service, and C-49b Hot Tub and Pool contractor classifications.
The official classification document lists C-19 Asbestos contractor, and DCCA renewal guidance requires C-19 responsible managing employees to provide asbestos-training proof.

limitations: Environmental notification, worker-safety, and training requirements may apply outside the contractor-license record.

evidence.note: The official classification document lists C-44 Sheet Metal and subclasses for gutters, awnings, and patio covers.
The official framework assigns pipelines and utility works to A General Engineering and lists C-43 Sewer, Sewage Disposal, Drain, and Pipe Laying plus C-43a Pipeline Repair.

limitations: Utility-owner approvals, county permits, and other environmental or right-of-way requirements are outside the contractor license source.

evidence.note: The official classification document grants B General Building contractors C-5 Cabinet, Millwork, and Carpentry Remodeling and Repairs scope and separately lists C-5 as a specialty classification.

limitations: The required classification depends on the trades and scope involved; this is not a separate Hawaii home-improvement registration program.

evidence.note: LSLBC publishes Building Construction as a major classification covering construction, maintenance, repair, development, and demolition of structures and associated site work.

limitations: The applicable license type, classification, and statutory project-value threshold must be evaluated for each project.

evidence.note: LSLBC defines Residential Construction as a major classification for qualifying residential structures and higher-value home-improvement work.

limitations: Manufactured housing mounted on a metal chassis and wheels is explicitly outside this classification.

evidence.note: LSLBC documents a statewide commercial contractor license for qualifying commercial projects and specialty-trade subcontracts.
LSLBC publishes Electrical as a major classification for qualifying electrical construction, installation, alteration, maintenance, and repair work.

limitations: The board states a $10,000 project-value threshold for this classification.

evidence.note: LSLBC publishes Plumbing as a credential classification and mechanical subclassification for qualifying potable, non-potable, and sewer-water systems within structures.

limitations: A mechanical contractor performing plumbing above the stated threshold must also possess a master plumber license from the State Plumbing Board of Louisiana.

evidence.note: LSLBC defines Heat, Air Conditioning, Ventilation, Duct Work, and Refrigeration as a Mechanical subclassification.

limitations: The board states a $10,000 project-value threshold for this work.

evidence.note: LSLBC publishes Mechanical as a major classification covering HVAC, refrigeration, hydronic and steam systems, pressure vessels, plumbing, gas and process piping, and mechanical equipment.
LSLBC publishes Roofing and Sheet Metal, Siding under Building Construction and a separate Residential Roofing subclassification.
LSLBC publishes Solar Energy Equipment for photovoltaic and thermal systems used for electricity or heating in houses, buildings, pools, and plumbing systems.

limitations: The solar classification requires an underlying Building Construction, Electrical, Mechanical, or Residential Construction classification and applies above the stated threshold.

evidence.note: LSLBC publishes Swimming Pools under Building Construction and Residential Swimming Pools for pools, whirlpools, hot tubs, water features, and related components.

limitations: Residential pool work has a separately stated $7,500 threshold and home-improvement registration alone does not authorize pool contracting.

evidence.note: LSLBC publishes Asbestos Removal and Abatement under Hazardous Materials and requires Louisiana Department of Environmental Quality asbestos certification.

limitations: The LSLBC classification is not a substitute for the required Department of Environmental Quality credential.

evidence.note: LSLBC's Roofing and Sheet Metal, Siding subclassification includes general sheet metal, while its Mechanical HVAC subclassification includes duct work.
LSLBC defines Municipal and Public Works Construction for water, sewer, gas, storm-water, power-plant, transmission-line, and underground electrical-conduit infrastructure.
LSLBC documents home-improvement registration as one of its basic credential paths and reports active home-improvement registrants.

limitations: Higher-value residential work moves into the Residential Construction license path, and home-improvement registration does not authorize residential pool work.

evidence.note: MSBOC publishes Building Construction as a major classification and states that a major classification covers its listed specialties.

limitations: A contractor may perform only work authorized by the classifications on its certificate, and other agency requirements may apply.

evidence.note: MSBOC defines Residential Builder and Residential Construction Manager classifications for qualifying residential construction, remodeling, repair, and improvement.

limitations: Residential Builder scope includes stated family-count, building-height, and commercial-square-foot limits.

evidence.note: The official licensing page identifies Building Construction and five other major commercial contractor classifications administered by MSBOC.
MSBOC publishes Electrical as a major classification and Residential Electrical as a separate residential classification.
MSBOC publishes Plumbing under Mechanical for commercial and residential systems and a separate Residential Plumbing classification.

limitations: The published plumbing classification excludes fuel-gas and boiler systems and identifies their applicable scopes separately.

evidence.note: MSBOC defines Heat, Ventilation, A/C - HVAC under Mechanical and a separate Residential HVAC classification.
MSBOC publishes Mechanical as a major classification covering heating, cooling, refrigeration, piping, ductwork, plumbing, boilers, pressure vessels, and related systems.
MSBOC publishes Roofing, Sheet Metal and Siding and a dedicated Residential Roofer classification.
MSBOC publishes stand-alone Solar and Wind Construction and a separate Residential Solar classification.

limitations: Contractors must also hold the appropriate electrical or mechanical classification for associated connections.

evidence.note: MSBOC's Swimming Pools specialty covers construction, repair, and servicing of swimming pools, hot tubs, and spas.

limitations: The classification excludes direct sanitary-sewer, potable-water, and electrical connections and excludes solar pool heating.

evidence.note: MSBOC's Hazardous Materials specialty explicitly includes asbestos abatement and directs contractors to the Mississippi Department of Environmental Quality for possible additional requirements.

limitations: This contractor-board classification does not replace any Department of Environmental Quality credential or project requirement.

evidence.note: MSBOC publishes both Roofing, Sheet Metal and Siding and a dedicated Sheetmetal specialty that includes fabrication, flashing, gutters, kitchen equipment, and ductwork.

MSBOC publishes Underground Utilities for water, sewer, and gas pipelines plus excavation, grading, paving, boring, and trenchless technologies, and Municipal and Public Works as a related major classification.
MSBOC defines Residential Remodeler for residential remodeling, repair, and improvement and includes improvement work within Residential Builder scope.

limitations: The residential classifications carry explicit building-height and scope limits; local permits remain outside this decision.

evidence.note: The official NCLBGC homepage states a general contractor must be licensed for projects valued at $40,000 or more and links the official contractor search.

limitations: Scope depends on the license classification and limitation shown by the board.

evidence.note: NCLBGC states the Residential Contractor classification covers construction and demolition activity for residential units and related ancillary site work.

limitations: Residential scope is tied to units required to conform to the residential building code and to the license limitation.

evidence.note: NCLBGC states the Building Contractor classification covers commercial, industrial, institutional, and residential building construction and demolition.

limitations: Commercial-building scope depends on classification and license limitation.

evidence.note: The official NCBEEC verify-license search lists North Carolina electrical contractor license classifications including limited, intermediate, unlimited, residential dwelling, and special restricted classifications.
The official plumbing/heating/fire-sprinkler board verify-license search lists plumbing contractor and technician classifications.
The official plumbing/heating/fire-sprinkler board verify-license search lists heating contractor and technician classifications.
The official refrigeration-board verify-license search lists commercial, industrial, service, and transport refrigeration contractor classifications.

limitations: This decision covers heating, fuel-piping, fire-sprinkler, and refrigeration-board mechanical-adjacent paths; unrelated mechanical scopes may require separate evidence.

evidence.note: Official NCLBGC solar-panel installation guidance says SPVP installations require an electrical contractor license and that NCLBGC scope may apply when structural or other general-contractor work is included.

limitations: Solar coverage is represented through electrical-contractor licensing and conditional general-contractor scope, not a standalone North Carolina solar contractor board.

evidence.note: NCLBGC states the Building and Residential classifications cover work under the S(Roofing) specialty classification.

limitations: Roofing coverage is represented through NCLBGC classification scope, not a standalone North Carolina roofing board.

evidence.note: NCLBGC states Building, Residential, and Public Utilities contractor classifications cover work under the S(Swimming Pools) specialty classification.
NCLBGC states the Building and Residential classifications cover work under the S(Asbestos) specialty classification.

limitations: Environmental notifications, training, or abatement rules may involve separate state or federal requirements outside the general-contractor board source.

evidence.note: NCLBGC states public utilities contractors cover water and sewer mains, service lines, treatment facilities, electrical power transmission and distribution ahead of delivery, communications distribution, and fuel-distribution facilities.

limitations: Public-utilities scope depends on subclassification and does not replace local permits or utility-owner requirements.

evidence.note: NCLBGC consumer guidance states a general contractor license is required for projects valued at $40,000 or more and links guidance for building, remodeling, and improving homes.

limitations: North Carolina home-improvement coverage is represented through general and residential contractor classifications and project-value thresholds, not a separate home-improvement contractor board.

evidence.note: 14.6.6 NMAC defines GB-98 General Building and fixed-works/general construction classifications issued by CID.

limitations: The exact license classification and qualifying-party scope must be preserved for verification.

evidence.note: 14.6.6 NMAC defines GB-2 Residential and GB-98 General Building classifications.
14.6.6 NMAC states GB-98 authorizes work on residential and commercial buildings and certain structures.
14.6.6 NMAC defines EE-98 residential, commercial, and industrial electrical wiring classifications and related electrical specialties.
14.6.6 NMAC defines MM-1 Plumbing and MM-98 Mechanical classifications.
14.6.6 NMAC defines MM-3 Heating, Ventilation & Air Conditioning and MM-98 Mechanical classifications.
The official CID bureau page lists mechanical and plumbing among the statewide construction-industry licensing bureaus.
14.6.6 NMAC defines GS-21 Roofing as a CID specialty classification.
14.6.6 NMAC includes solar thermal energy systems in MM-1 and MM-4 scopes, solar air handling applications in MM-3, and limited solar photovoltaic arrays in water-well pump electrical scopes.

limitations: Future normalization must preserve whether solar work is photovoltaic, solar thermal, HVAC-related, plumbing-related, or incidental to another classification.

evidence.note: 14.6.6 NMAC defines GS-25 Swimming Pools and includes swimming pools and spas in the MM-1 plumbing scope.
The official NMED asbestos page states contractors who remove asbestos materials must be licensed by the New Mexico Construction Industries Division.

limitations: Asbestos environmental compliance and notification requirements may involve NMED or local air-quality authorities in addition to CID licensing.

evidence.note: 14.6.6 NMAC defines GS-32 Miscellaneous Sheet Metal and a JSM journeyman sheet metal credential under mechanical/plumbing classifications.
14.6.6 NMAC defines GF-9 Utility Lines and Systems for sewage, water, natural gas, and underground telephone cables.
14.6.6 NMAC defines residential and general building classifications that include erecting, altering, repairing, and demolishing residential and commercial buildings.

limitations: Local permits and business registrations remain outside this statewide CID source.

evidence.note: The official NSCB classification page lists Class A General Engineering, Class B General Building, and Class AB contractor classifications.

limitations: Scope depends on license classification, subclassification, and monetary limit.

evidence.note: The official NSCB classification page lists B-2 Residential and Small Commercial and B-7 Residential Remodeling under General Building.

limitations: Residential recovery-fund and disclosure requirements are not modeled as separate verification records in this source entry.

evidence.note: The official NSCB classification page lists General Building classifications including B-6 Commercial Remodeling and general engineering/building classes.

limitations: The specific commercial scope must be read from the license classification and monetary limit.

evidence.note: NAC Chapter 624 includes Classification C-2 for Electrical contracting and subclassifications.
NAC Chapter 624 includes Classification C-1 for Plumbing and heating contracting and subclassifications.
NAC Chapter 624 includes Classification C-21 for Refrigeration and air-conditioning and subclassifications.

NAC Chapter 624 includes mechanical-adjacent scopes such as C-1 plumbing/heating, C-21 refrigeration and air-conditioning, C-38 gas equipment, C-39 heaters, C-41 fire protection, and A-20 industrial piping.

limitations: Mechanical coverage is represented through Nevada's separate contractor classifications rather than one unified mechanical license.

evidence.note: NAC Chapter 624 includes Classification C-15 for Roofing and siding and subclassifications.
NAC Chapter 624 includes Classification C-37 for Solar contracting and subclassifications.

limitations: Electrical or mechanical companion classifications may apply depending on the solar installation scope.

evidence.note: The official NSCB classification page lists A-10 Commercial and Residential Pools and links consumer guidance for contracting for a pool or spa.
The official NSCB classification page lists A-23 Removal of Asbestos under General Engineering.

limitations: Environmental, workplace-safety, or notification requirements may apply outside the contractor-board classification.

evidence.note: NAC Chapter 624 includes Classification C-13 for using sheet metal and C-21c sheet metal within refrigeration and air-conditioning.
The official NSCB classification page lists A-15 sewers, drains and pipes, A-17 lines to transmit electricity, and A-19 pipeline and conduits.
The official NSCB classification page lists B-7 Residential Remodeling and C-3 Carpentry, maintenance and minor repairs is included in NAC Chapter 624.

limitations: Handyman exemptions, local permits, and disclosure requirements must be preserved as caveats; this is not a municipal-license decision.

evidence.note: Title 40 Chapter 11 defines general construction and the statewide General Contractors-Building classification.

limitations: The exact classification, subclassification, license group, and statutory project-value threshold must be evaluated for each project.

evidence.note: The Residential Builders Commission defines its statewide builder license for qualifying residential construction, repair, improvement, and reimprovement.

limitations: The residential builder scope is limited by building height, unit count, project value, and mechanical-trade qualifications described by the commission.

evidence.note: The Contractor's Licensing Board states that regulated commercial general or mechanical construction above the statutory threshold requires its license.
Section 40-11-410 defines the commercial mechanical-electrical subclassification, while the Residential Builders Commission separately licenses residential electrical contractors.

limitations: Commercial and residential electrical credentials are maintained through separate board paths.

evidence.note: The commission lists a Residential Specialty Plumbing License, while Title 40 Chapter 11 defines plumbing as a commercial mechanical subclassification.

limitations: Commercial and residential plumbing credentials are maintained through separate board paths.

evidence.note: The commission lists a Residential Specialty Heating and Air License, while Title 40 Chapter 11 defines air-conditioning, heating, packaged-equipment, and refrigeration subclassifications.

limitations: Commercial and residential HVAC credentials are maintained through separate board paths.

evidence.note: Title 40 Chapter 11 defines mechanical construction and its air-conditioning, heating, packaged-equipment, electrical, plumbing, piping, and refrigeration subclassifications.
Section 40-11-410 defines the statewide commercial roofing subclassification; the Residential Builders Commission separately lists roofing specialty registration.

Section 40-11-410 assigns commercial solar component installation, roof mounting, structural upgrades, and wiring to specified general or mechanical classifications; the residential commission lists solar specialty registration.

limitations: A solar project may require multiple classifications for mounting, structural work, and electrical connections.

evidence.note: Section 40-11-410 defines Swimming Pools for residential, commercial, and institutional pools and spas and their integral equipment.

limitations: South Carolina keeps swimming-pool contracting in the Contractor's Licensing Board path rather than the general residential-builder path.

evidence.note: The DES Asbestos Section states that it licenses contractors and individuals who handle regulated asbestos-containing material and publishes an official contractor search.

limitations: DES asbestos licensing is separate from LLR contractor classifications and must be verified through the asbestos-specific source.

evidence.note: Section 40-11-410 includes ducts and ventilation work in mechanical subclassifications and sheet-metal valleys, flashings, gutters, and related work in roofing.

limitations: Future normalization must distinguish HVAC duct work from roofing-related sheet metal and preserve the applicable subclassification.

evidence.note: Section 40-11-410 defines public-utility classifications for pipelines, water and sewer plants and lines, boring and tunneling, and underground electrical systems.
The Residential Builders Commission defines specialty contracting for qualifying residential construction, repairs, improvement, and reimprovement and publishes the available trade registrations.

limitations: Specialty registration is limited in classification count and scope and does not authorize work outside the registered trades.

evidence.note: The Tennessee Board for Licensing Contractors states that it regulates contractors under Title 62, Chapter 6.

limitations: Licensing is tied to classification, monetary limit, and statutory project thresholds.

evidence.note: The official classification outline defines Residential BC-A and Limited Residential BC-A/r contractor classifications for residential construction, remodeling, repair, and improvement.

limitations: BC-A/r has a single-family and monetary-limit restriction; home-improvement coverage is county-specific.

evidence.note: The official classification outline defines Commercial BC-B, Small Commercial BC-b(sm), Industrial BC-C, and combined BC classifications.

limitations: Commercial and industrial scopes depend on the classification and monetary limit printed on the license.

evidence.note: The official classification outline defines Electrical Contracting CE and says the CE designation covers all electrical categories for residential, commercial, and industrial projects.

limitations: Limited Licensed Electrician records are also administered by the board; alarm-system work may require separate board treatment.

evidence.note: The official classification outline defines Mechanical Contracting CMC-A for plumbing and gas piping, and the board page identifies Limited Licensed Plumbers.

limitations: Local permits and inspections may still be required; contractor and limited-plumber paths should not be merged without preserving license type.

evidence.note: The official classification outline defines CMC-C HVAC, refrigeration, and gas piping, including Solar HVAC (Geothermal).

limitations: Geothermal well-driller requirements may involve TDEC and must be surfaced as companion requirements.

evidence.note: The official classification outline defines Mechanical Contracting CMC and subcategories for plumbing, process piping, HVAC, sprinklers, insulation, pollution control, pneumatic tube systems, controls, boilers, and fuel gas.

limitations: Fire-protection, boiler, and geothermal work may require companion agency permits or credentials.

evidence.note: The official classification outline lists Roofing BC-21 and notes roofing prime and subcontractors are required to be licensed at the stated threshold.
The official classification outline lists S-Solar Panel Installations and identifies Solar HVAC (Geothermal) under CMC-C.

limitations: Electrical, HVAC, or other underlying classifications may determine the required license for a specific solar project.

evidence.note: The official classification outline lists Swimming Pools as BC-26 within the building categories.
The official classification outline lists Specialty/Environmental S-A Asbestos Material Handling/Removing.

limitations: The outline directs users to TDEC for asbestos-related accreditation, training, or additional information; that companion source should be registered before adapter work.

evidence.note: The official classification outline lists Sheet Metal BC-20 within the building categories.
The official classification outline defines Municipal and Utility Construction MU, including underground piping for gas, sewer, waterlines, and underground conduit.

limitations: Some utility and environmental work may also require TDOT, TDEC, or local permits.

evidence.note: The official board page states that the board regulates Home Improvement Contractors.

limitations: Tennessee home-improvement licensing has county-specific applicability and project-value thresholds that must be preserved in verification wording.

evidence.note: The Governor's Small Business Handbook says general contracting, construction, and home-builder licensing is generally handled at the local or city level and directs businesses to the relevant municipality.

limitations: Texas still licenses specific trades such as electrical, air conditioning and refrigeration, plumbing, and asbestos through separate statewide agencies.
Municipal and county contractor licensing remains outside OpenTrade Registry v2 board-completion scope unless separately registered.

evidence.note: The Governor's Small Business Handbook groups home builders with general contracting and construction and says licensing, permitting, or bonding requirements are generally local or municipal.

limitations: This decision covers the absence of a verified statewide residential or home-builder contractor board; trade-specific state licenses and local permits may still apply.

evidence.note: The official Texas Business Licenses and Permits Guide says general contractors are not required to obtain a state license and may instead be subject to county or city requirements.

limitations: Separate statewide licenses remain necessary when commercial work includes a regulated trade or activity.
This decision does not represent local commercial contractor registrations or project permits.

evidence.note: TDLR Electrical Safety and Licensing FAQ states that anyone who performs electrical work in Texas must be licensed and identifies contractor license paths.
The Texas State Board of Plumbing Examiners lists statewide plumbing license types including Tradesman Plumber-Limited, Journeyman Plumber, Master Plumber, and Plumbing Inspector.
TDLR states that contractors who install, repair, or maintain air conditioning, refrigeration, or heating systems must have a TDLR license.
TDLR Air Conditioning and Refrigeration contractor licensing documentation says license class and endorsements determine the type of air conditioning and refrigeration work that may be performed.

limitations: This decision covers the TDLR ACR mechanical/HVAC path and does not resolve unrelated mechanical scopes outside TDLR ACR licensing.

evidence.note: The official Governor's Small Business webinar transcript states that Texas does not have a statewide roofing license requirement.

limitations: Local permits, registrations, insurance rules, and trade-specific licenses may still apply to roofing projects.
Voluntary industry certifications are not treated as state licenses.

evidence.note: TDLR consumer guidance for solar panels says licensed electrical contractors perform solar installation work and must hold or employ a master electrician.

limitations: This decision covers solar installation through the electrical-contractor licensing path; residential solar retail sales registration is a separate future TDLR program and is not treated as active contractor-license coverage here.

evidence.note: DSHS states persons and companies engaging in asbestos-related activities in a public building in Texas must be appropriately licensed or registered by DSHS.

limitations: DSHS asbestos scope is public-building asbestos-program licensing and registration; this is not a statewide general contractor, demolition-permit, or local building-permit source.

evidence.note: The Governor's Small Business Handbook directs general contracting, construction, and home-builder businesses to local or city licensing, permitting, and bonding requirements rather than a statewide contractor board.

limitations: This decision concerns statewide contractor licensing and does not negate trade-specific state credentials, consumer-protection law, or local home-improvement requirements.

evidence.note: DOPL lists B100 General Contractor and E100 General Engineering Contractor among Utah general contractor classifications.

limitations: The license classification and qualifier requirements determine what work a contractor may perform.

evidence.note: DOPL lists R100 Residential/Small Commercial Contractor among Utah general contractor classifications.
DOPL lists B100 General Contractor, R100 Residential/Small Commercial Contractor, and E100 General Engineering Contractor classifications.
DOPL lists E200 General Electrical Contractor and E201 Residential Electrical Contractor and states electrical contractors must have a Master Electrical qualifier.
DOPL lists P200 General Plumbing Contractor and P201 Residential Plumbing Contractor and states plumbing contractors must have a Master Plumber qualifier.
DOPL announces H100 HVAC Contractor licensing requirements, including the HVAC exam and HVAC-specific experience.
The Utah Construction Trades Licensing Act Rule identifies mechanical-relevant classifications including HVAC and boiler, pipeline, wastewater, and water conditioner work.

limitations: Utah mechanical coverage is represented through separate classifications rather than one unified mechanical license.

evidence.note: DOPL's official classification conversion chart identifies S280 Roofing Contractor.
DOPL's official classification conversion chart identifies S202 Solar Photovoltaic Contractor and solar-thermal work within the converted S410 classification.

limitations: Electrical or plumbing/HVAC companion requirements may apply depending on the solar installation scope.

evidence.note: DOPL's official classification conversion chart maps Swimming Pool and Spa Contractor work into the S330 Landscape and Recreation Contractor classification.
The Utah Construction Trades Licensing Act Rule states asbestos removal regulated by the Department of Environmental Quality does not require a contractor license under this rule.

limitations: This is not a no-regulation finding; DEQ-regulated asbestos evidence should be registered separately before adapter work.

evidence.note: DOPL's official classification conversion chart identifies prior S340 Sheet Metal Contractor coverage and maps that classification into current S220 Carpentry and Flooring Contractor coverage.

limitations: Sheet-metal verification must preserve the DOPL classification shown on the license record because current coverage may appear under the converted classification rather than a standalone sheet-metal code.

evidence.note: DOPL's official classification conversion chart identifies S410 Boiler, Pipeline, Waste Water, and Water Conditioner Contractor for pipeline, conduit, sewer, wastewater, and related work.
DOPL's contractor application identifies R101 Residential/Small Commercial Non-Structural Remodeling and Repair alongside R100 residential/small commercial classifications.

limitations: Local permits and business licenses remain outside this statewide DOPL contractor source.
